paragraphs.en: What Happened to Your Face?
paragraphs.zh: 你的脸发生了什么？

paragraphs.en: How the human countenance became something to study, edit, optimize, and scan.
paragraphs.zh: 人的面部表情和轮廓从一个可被观察的人性特征，变成了需要被研究、编辑、优化和扫描的对象。

paragraphs.en: By Cal Revely-Calder
paragraphs.zh: 作者：Cal Revely-Calder

paragraphs.en: The physiognomists promised that your character could be read from your features. Certain forms of facial-recognition technology have revived that old fantasy in digital form.Illustration by Bryce Wymer
paragraphs.zh: 面相学家曾承诺说，一个人的性格可以从五官特征中读出来。某些形式的面部识别技术以数字化方式复活了这种老旧幻想。图解：Bryce Wymer

paragraphs.en: Several months ago, my partner and I bought an apartment in South London. Our previous home was a rental in which, for reasons best known to the landlord, there were mirrors everywhere. The bathroom had two; there was one outside on the terrace; in the bedroom, mirrored panels stretched across a twenty-foot-long wall. On moving day, we realized that we had a problem: the new apartment was mirror-free, and because we’d been so spoiled we weren’t bringing one of our own. We spent a few days filling our drafty rooms, decanting books, building furniture, and dressing every morning without seeing ourselves in profile. It was a couple of weeks before we bought a simple mirror, wooden and round, to hang above the bathroom sink. By then, I joked, we didn’t recognize ourselves.
paragraphs.zh: 几个月前，我和伴侣在伦敦南部买了一套公寓。我们之前住的租房子里，出于房东不为人知的原因，镜子到处都是。浴室里有两个，露台外也有一个，卧室里则是延伸到整面约20英尺长墙面的镜面护壁。搬家那天，我们意识到有个问题：新公寓是“无镜子”的，而且我们已经被以前的生活宠坏了，没有带自己的一面镜子。接下来的几天，我们只是在通风漏风的房间里忙碌，倒出书籍、搭建家具，每天早上照着镜子都不行，只能不看自己侧面地穿衣服。过了两个星期，我们终于在浴室洗手池上方挂上了一面简洁的木框圆镜。到那时，我开玩笑说，我们已经认不出自己了。

paragraphs.en: As my feeble quip indicates, we take for granted that we possess a steady mental image of our own faces, and that we can reconfirm these images frequently. If I asked you to picture yourself, you could almost certainly do this with ease. You might refer to the memory of how you looked in the mirror this morning, or in the selfies you took last night, or in that passport photo you hate. And if I suggested that you double-check your mental image, you could probably do so by looking in the mirror again, or at your reflection in a nearby window, or, via a camera app, using the very screen on which you may be reading this paragraph. Most of the time, we think we know how we look.
paragraphs.zh: 正如我那句蹩脚玩笑所示，我们习惯了认为自己拥有一幅稳定的自我面容心像，并且可以随时把它重新确认。如果我让你想象一下你自己，你几乎肯定能轻松做到。你可能会回忆起今天早上照镜子时的样子，或者昨晚拍下的自拍，或是那张你讨厌的护照照片。如果我建议你再把那幅心像核对一遍，你也大概会再照镜子一遍，或对着附近窗户里的倒影，或者通过相机应用——也就是你可能正在阅读这段文字的屏幕——来检查。大多数时候，我们确实认为自己知道自己长什么样。

paragraphs.en: Discover notable new fiction, nonfiction, and poetry.
paragraphs.zh: 探索值得关注的新小说、非虚构与诗歌。

paragraphs.en: Yet that’s possible only because we live in the aftermath of a revolution. In the past few centuries, the mental self-portrait has become commonplace. To be sure, the world has held pockets of reflection as long as humans have existed to see them. As a piece of technology, the mirror seems to date back several millennia. The ancient Egyptians had polished bronze, copper, and obsidian at their disposal, as did the Greeks and the Romans. But such objects were hardly ordinary, and people had to improvise: in Ovid’s Metamorphoses, Narcissus, the ultimate lover of reflections, sees himself for the first time in a crystalline pool. Pausanias, writing a century later, called this scene “totally absurd,” because any young man would have seen a reflection before. Still, for thousands of years, there was an obvious gap between image and life. Now in a kitchen pot, darkly; now face to face.
paragraphs.zh: 但这一切之所以可能，完全是因为我们生活在一场革命之后。就在过去几个世纪里，自我面貌的心理定格照才变得普及。可以肯定，人类只要存在，就一直有反射的场景。作为一门技术，镜子至少可以追溯到几千年前。古埃及人会用抛光的青铜、铜和黑曜石，希腊人和罗马人也是如此。但这类物件并不寻常，人们不得不即兴应对：在《变形记》里，沉迷于反射的终极人物那索斯（Narcissus）第一次在一汪晶亮的水塘里看到自己。一个多世纪后，帕乌萨尼亚（Pausanias）称这一情节“完全荒谬”，因为任何年轻人都会见过自己的倒影。尽管如此，数千年来，影像与真实之间始终有着明显的距离。或是在一口黑乎乎的厨房锅里，或是面对面。

paragraphs.en: The revolution came in the sixteenth century, when Venice’s glassmakers applied a tin-and-mercury alloy to their panes, producing a far clearer reflection than had previously been achieved. These artisans, with their trade secrets, were kept on Murano, a cluster of islands just across the lagoon from the city, ostensibly as a precaution against fire, though the state would also send assassins after anyone who tried to leave. Still, a few hardy glassmakers escaped to the Saint-Gobain glass workshop in Paris, which they lifted to pan-European dominance. The workshop gave us, among other monuments to subtlety, the great Galerie des Glaces in the Palace of Versailles, which contains three hundred and sixty-seven mirrors, stretching eighty yards. The technology could be replicated, spread, sold. Mirrors quickly infiltrated the private homes of the Continent, then its colonies; they became cheap and unremarkable, as they remain today, if you stop shelving your books long enough to buy one.
paragraphs.zh: 这场革命发生在16世纪，当时威尼斯的玻璃匠在玻璃板上覆上一层锡汞合金，得到了此前从未达到过的更清晰的反射。由于掌握着手艺秘密，这些工匠被安排住在穆拉诺岛群（在城市对岸的潟湖彼端），名义上是为了防火，实际上有人企图离开就会被国家派杀手追捕。尽管如此，仍有少数顽强的玻璃工人逃到了巴黎圣戈班的玻璃作坊，而他们把那家作坊推向了泛欧洲的主导地位。该作坊留下了诸多精湛工艺的代表作，其中最著名的是凡尔赛宫中的镜廊（Galerie des Glaces），里面有367面镜子，延绵80码。该技术很快被复制、传播并售卖。镜子迅速进入欧洲大陆私人住宅，随后又进了其殖民地；只要你放下书架上的书，掏出钱买下一面镜子，它们就会变得便宜而普通，正如今天一样。

paragraphs.en: In the centuries that followed, the private face became increasingly public. More people could look at their own countenances more accurately, and more often. Candlelight and gaslight gave way to electricity, an unwobbling source of interior light. Photography arrived, kindling an enthusiasm that Baudelaire, ever the cynic, compared to a lust for pornography. Cosmetics, as venerable as mirrors, boomed to an unprecedented degree. Think of Esther marinating her skin in myrrh, then of Eugène Rimmel, who introduced the first mass-produced mascara via an early mail-order catalogue. Pierre-François-Pascal Guerlain, a trained chemist, sold skin creams with such aplomb that he was hired by Napoleon III’s empress, Eugénie. Fast-forward to the twenty-first century: to fix your flesh, there’s Botox, fillers, and plastic surgery; to augment the image, there are camera-app filters and Photoshop. In 2024, according to Apple, the world’s iPhone users took five hundred billion selfies. Facial-recognition technology is used across the globe. In the span of a few centuries, the face has become inescapable. We seem to be condemned to look our best.
paragraphs.zh: 在其后的几个世纪里，私人的面孔变得越来越公共。越来越多人可以更准确、更频繁地观看自己的容貌。蜡烛灯和煤气灯让位于电力——一种稳定不抖的室内光源。摄影术到来时，激起了热情，连巴德莱尔这个彻头彻尾的讽刺主义者都把它比作对色情的欲望。护肤和化妆，和镜子一样古老，前所未有地繁荣起来。想想艾丝帖尔用没药去“浸泡”皮肤；再想想欧仁·里梅尔（Eugène Rimmel）通过早期邮购目录推出首个大规模生产的睫毛膏。受过训练的化学家皮埃尔-弗朗索瓦-帕斯卡尔·格兰兰（Pierre-François-Pascal Guerlain）卖出的护肤霜卖得如此风生水起，以至于拿破仑三世的皇后奥热妮（Eugénie）请他任用。快进到21世纪：想修复皮肤，可以用肉毒杆菌素、填充剂和整形手术；想增强形象，可以用相机滤镜和Photoshop。2024年，据苹果称，全球iPhone用户拍下了五千亿张自拍。人脸识别技术已在世界各地广泛应用。几个世纪间，脸已变成不可回避的存在。我们仿佛注定要把自己打扮得尽可能好看。

paragraphs.en: Much of our social life, our ways of making and sharing a world, orbits the face. Emmanuel Levinas, the supreme theorist of facial affairs, described our fleshy ovals as the site where ethics—his “first philosophy”—begins. This rings true when it comes to everyday behavior. The phenomenon may be hardwired: babies prefer faces to other complex objects. And, for the rest of our lives, faces seem charged by something that arms or legs, however attractive, don’t have. When you talk to me, I look at your eyes, regardless of whether you’re waving your hands. If the gaze is held too long, one of us has to look away. If I can’t see your face—say, you turn aside, or you’ve called me on the phone—I can get by, can make the conversation work, but I might feel that our connection is lacking. The face’s centrality to social life is inscribed in our language, in the metaphors that betray our world view. In English, we face up to things, or face them down; we show our faces where required; we try to save face, and should that fail we make an about-face to save ourselves.
paragraphs.zh: 我们大量的社交生活、构建并共享世界的方式，都绕着脸运行。被视为“面部伦理”最高思想家之一的艾玛纽埃尔·列维纳斯称我们的“肉质椭圆”是“第一哲学”——即伦理——的起点。放到日常行为上，这很有道理。婴儿偏爱脸部而不是其他复杂对象，这种现象似乎是先天的。此后的一生中，脸又带着某种力量，手和脚，即便多么吸引人，也没有这种力量。当你和我交谈时，我会看你的眼睛，不管你是否在挥手。如果对方的凝视持续太久，两人中总有人会移开视线。如果我看不到你的脸，比如你转过身去，或你在打电话叫我，我还是能应付，会让谈话继续，但我可能会觉得彼此缺了些什么。脸在社交生活中的核心性写进了我们的语言，写进那些暴露我们世界观的隐喻里。英语里我们会正面对待某件事，或面对困难；需要时要“露面”；我们努力“保住面子”，若失败了就会转身来拯救自己。

paragraphs.en: Yet there’s another side to this coin. Our vocabulary also speaks of invention, even artifice. The English word “face” derives from the Latin facies, implying a created form; so does the French visage, from videre, suggesting something seen from without. “Mask,” “masque,” “mascara,” “maquillage,” and their European relations seem to be etymologically linked, and carry long-standing associations with concealment, distortion, pretense. For Socrates, the art worth prizing was the cultivation and preservation of natural beauty; we’ve happily overwritten it with what he treated suspiciously as kommōtikē, the art of changing how one looks. Even “person,” along with “impersonation” and “personae,” derives from persona, a theatrical mask through which classical actors spoke. The cultural historian Hans Belting suggested that faces and masks were conceptually inseparable: we shouldn’t think of one as “real” and the other as “fake”—one as the thing we have and the other as the thing we temporarily don. Life, he wrote, was fully “a perpetuum mobile,” an “expressive drama,” in which our faces resolve into one legible position, one legible role, then reassemble themselves into the next. We make them up, in every sense.
paragraphs.zh: 但这枚硬币还有另一面。我们的词汇里也谈到创造，甚至谈到伪饰。英语单词“face”来自拉丁语“facies”，含有“人为塑成的形态”之意；法语“visage”也从“videre”而来，指“被外部看到的样子”。“mask”“masque”“mascara”“maquillage”及其欧洲语系词语在词源上似乎相通，并长期带着隐匿、扭曲、装饰、伪装的意味。对苏格拉底而言，值得珍视的艺术是培养并保持天然之美；我们则乐于把它改写为他所怀疑的“kommōtikē”——改变外表的艺术。连“person”与“impersonation”“personae”一样，都来自persona，即古典戏剧演员表演时所戴的面具。文化史学者汉斯·贝尔廷（Hans Belting）曾指出，面孔与面具在观念上本是不可分割的：我们不应把一个当“真实”，另一个当“虚假”——一个是我们拥有的东西，另一个是我们暂时“借用”或“戴上”的东西。他写道，生活本身是一部真正的“永动机”，一场“表现性戏剧”：我们的面孔先定格为一个可辨认的位置、一个可辨认的角色，接着又重组为下一个。我们在各个层面都在“制作”它们。

paragraphs.en: The face, then, lives by its pliability. We might encounter a completed sentence or picture, but never a completed face. It can always shift into something else, pushed by personal whim or social pressure, and keep doing so until frozen into one last mask by sickness or death. This means that we live together in an interpretative flux. We constantly read other faces, and understand that ours, at the same time, will be interpreted. As T. S. Eliot put it, we prepare a face to meet the faces that we meet, and we do so knowing that they’ve been prepared as well, and that our interpretations will almost immediately become obsolete.
paragraphs.zh: 面孔，因此依赖于其可塑性。我们也许会遇到一段写完的句子或一幅完成的图片，却永远见不到一张“完成”的脸。它总是可以被个人心血来潮或社会压力推着变成别的样子，并且会不断变化，直到在疾病或死亡面前被凝固成最后一副面具。这意味着我们共同生活在一种解释的流动之中。我们不断解读他人的面孔，同时也明白，我们自己的面孔也正在被解读。正如 T. S. Eliot 所说，我们为要见到的面孔准备着一张面孔，也清楚对方也同样有备而来，而我们的这些解释几乎会立刻变得过时。

paragraphs.en: “O.K., great—let’s break for now and continue the indecision, procrastination, and blame-shifting over e-mail.”
paragraphs.zh: “好吧，太好了——那我们先暂停一下，之后再通过电子邮件继续拖延、再拖延、再推卸责任。”

paragraphs.en: Levinas tends to be the philosophical lodestar here, with his near-mystical love of the face-to-face encounter. He wrote that the face was “a living presence,” that “the Other faces me and puts me in question and obliges me.” Obliged him to do what? This may not be clear. Encountering a face means reëncountering the world: it is a silent epiphany about the fact that other people are radically outside of you, maybe unknowable in a final sense, and yet you must engage with them anyway.
paragraphs.zh: 在这一点上，列维纳斯常常成为哲学方向上的参照星，他几乎带有神秘色彩地热爱“面对面”相遇。他写道，面孔是“一种鲜活的在场”，并且“他者的脸朝向我，使我陷入被质询的处境，并迫使我行动”。它究竟迫使人做什么？这也许并不清楚。遭遇一张脸意味着重新遇见世界：它是一次沉默的顿悟，提醒我们他人以一种根本性的方式处于你之外，或许在终极意义上无法被彻底认知，但无论如何你都必须与之互动。

paragraphs.en: At the same time, I prefer Ludwig Wittgenstein. He was more impish than people remember, and he liked to draw little faces when he was teaching or writing, sketching each one in a few strokes and usually making it smile. Such doodles recur in his manuscripts from the early nineteen-thirties until shortly before his death, in 1951; he used them to illustrate a battery of subtle points, one of which was how misleading our language could be. Here’s one he drew for his students:
paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，我更偏爱路德维希·维特根斯坦。他比人们记忆中的样子更顽皮，在教学或写作时喜欢画些小小的人脸图，常常用几笔勾勒并通常让它们带着笑。这些速写从1930年代早期一直持续到他去世前不久（1951年）；他用它们来说明许多微妙观点，其中之一是我们的语言有多么容易误导人。下面是他给学生画的一张。

paragraphs.en: After which he said:
paragraphs.zh: 在这之后他说：

paragraphs.en: One of the things Wittgenstein implies is that we don’t theorize the faces we see. I might talk of “reading” your face, but it isn’t like reading a code or a map: I don’t look at you, add together your liquid eyes and droopy mouth, and pronounce my final judgment that you’re sad. I simply look at your face, apprehend its gestalt as a unity, and go from there. Recent neuroscientific research has shown that we process whole faces more readily than mere parts; we aren’t adding the bits up. We look, and we speak, and we look again: that’s life.
paragraphs.zh: 维特根斯坦所暗示的其中一件事是：我们不会对看到的面孔进行“理论化”处理。我可能会说“读你的脸”，但这并不像读一段代码或一张地图：我不是看着你，把你湿润的眼睛和下垂的嘴拼在一起，最后断言你是悲伤的。我只是看你的脸，把它作为一个统一整体把握住，然后从那里开始。近期的神经科学研究表明，我们更容易处理完整的面孔，而不是仅有的零散部件；我们并不是把每一小块相加。我们看，我们说，我们再看——这就是生活。

paragraphs.en: One oddity, given all this, is that, although humans have made pictures of faces for as long as we’ve represented anything, realism has only recently become the norm. As the scholar Fay Bound-Alberti points out in “The Face: A Cultural History” (Grand Central), between the thirty-thousand-year-old figures on the walls of the Chauvet-Pont d’Arc cave and ancient Cycladic statuary, from the fourth to second millennium B.C., human figures rarely showed recognizable facial features. There were exceptions. In Egypt, certain pharaohs seemed, for reasons still debated, to tilt their own representations from the ideal to the natural. Senusret III, the most outlandish example, had temples filled with statues of himself, his gigantic faces lined and grim and aged, though set atop a young and muscular body. Centuries later came an interlude in Greece and Rome, beginning with the so-called Severe style of Greek sculpture, in which faces gained clearer features and bodies sharper definition. Socrates’ ugliness can be reliably inferred from his many unsexy sculptural representations; the statues and busts of individual Caesars, though they blend truth and propaganda, remain easy to distinguish. But this facial gallery closed with the Western Roman Empire and the rise of Christianity, which inherited a suspicion of idolatry from the Old Testament and Judaism. David Le Breton points out in “Faces” (Polity) that the Apostolic Constitutions, a fourth-century collection of Christian ecclesiastical laws, recommended that painters be exiled altogether from Christendom, just as poets would have been from Plato’s ideal state. They stayed, but, rather than memorializing individual heroes, they largely devoted themselves to religious imagery, in which realism was again forgone in favor of iconic value. The old order had resumed.
paragraphs.zh: 鉴于此有一处反常：虽然人类从最早能够表现任何事物开始就画过面孔，但写实主义最近才成为常态。学者Fay Bound-Alberti在《The Face: A Cultural History》(Grand Central) 中指出，在上万年前夏乌维特—蓬·达尔克洞穴墙上的人物到公元前第四到第二千纪的古代基克拉底雕像中，人形很少展现出可识别的面部特征。也有例外。在埃及，某些法老似乎出于至今仍有争议的原因，将自己的形象从理想化转向更自然。塞努斯雷特三世是最离奇的例子，他在神庙中立有数不清的自己雕像：脸庞巨大、棱角分明、严峻而衰老，却坐落在年轻而健壮的身体上。几个世纪后，在希腊和罗马出现了一个插曲，从所谓的希腊“严肃风格”雕塑开始，面孔逐渐拥有更清晰的特征，身体也更清楚地成形。苏格拉底的难看可以从他众多不性感的雕塑再现中可靠地推断出来；即使诸位凯撒的雕像与半身像往往在真实与宣传之间摇摆，也仍然易于辨认。可是，这座面孔长廊在西罗马帝国和基督教兴起时结束。基督教从旧约和犹太教中继承了对偶像崇拜的疑惧。David Le Breton在《Faces》(Polity) 中指出，第四世纪基督教教会法典《使徒法则》曾建议将画家从基督教世界中逐出，正如柏拉图理想国会将诗人逐出一样。他们确实留了下来，但不再纪念个体英雄，而是主要投入宗教意象创作；在这些创作中，现实主义再次被抛开，取而代之的是象征性的价值。旧秩序又恢复了。

paragraphs.en: Only with the Renaissance would it break again, with the fifteenth-century blossoming of the naturalistic portrait: van Eyck, Campin, Piero della Francesca. The archetype of painted interiority may still be the good old “Mona Lisa,” known in Italian as “La Gioconda”—the face that launched a thousand conjectures. Bound-Alberti brings her in, dutifully, to say how “enigmatic” her smile is, though Wittgenstein, again, made more of the cliché: “When we speak of the enigmatic smile of the Mona Lisa, that may well mean that we ask ourselves: In what situation, in what story, might one smile like that?” To talk of stories, he means, is to talk of time; you need the before and the after, the rounded sense of a life. After all, some people laugh in anger and weep with joy. If I read your smile as happiness, I’m drawing on a fluid sense of the character I know, and on our place in an emotional culture. One reason Leonardo’s painting has endured in the popular imagination is straightforward: we want to ask questions of her—how she feels, what she’s thinking—and though we don’t believe she’s alive any more than we believe Hamlet or James Bond to be alive, we can’t help responding to her face as if she were.
paragraphs.zh: 直到文艺复兴才再次被打破，出现在十五世纪自然主义肖像的繁荣时期：凡·艾克、坎平、皮罗·德拉·方查。绘画“内在性”原型或许仍是那幅经典的《蒙娜丽莎》，意大利语里称为“La Gioconda”——这张诞生了无数猜测的面孔。Bound-Alberti也按例把她拿来说明她的笑容有多“神秘”，尽管维特根斯坦又一次把陈词滥调讲透了：“当我们说到蒙娜丽莎的神秘微笑时，这很可能意味着我们在追问：在什么情境、什么故事里，一个人会这样微笑？”他说到故事，其实就是在说时间；你需要前因后果，需要生命的圆融感。毕竟，有些人发怒时会笑，有些人喜极而泣。如果我把你的笑容读作快乐，我是在借助我对你性格的流动式感知，以及我们所处的情绪文化。列奥纳多的这幅画之所以在大众想象中得以长久流传，原因很直接：我们想向她提问——她在想什么、她在感受什么——虽然我们并不真相信她还活着，就像我们不认为哈姆雷特或詹姆斯·邦德还活着一样，但我们还是忍不住像对待真实之人那样回应她的面孔。

paragraphs.en: One unexpected consequence of the return of realism was a renewed attention to anatomy. Take dissection, which was mostly in abeyance through the medieval era, though by the thirteenth century in Europe it was beginning to appear in university curricula. During the Renaissance, it flourished again, and became a routine part of an artist’s studies, as Leonardo’s sketches attest. At the same time, another art returned, equally venerable and equally obsessed with the human form: physiognomy. The thinking, from the Greeks onward, was that you could, if sufficiently skilled, read people’s inward rottenness from their outward appearance. They might alter their behavior or speech, but a nose could never lie. Physiognomic treatises reappeared in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, then took off in the eighteenth and nineteenth. The star was Johann Kaspar Lavater, a Swiss poet and philosopher, whose “Physiognomische Fragmente,” a complete manual of flesh-reading, was published in four volumes between 1775 and 1778, featuring illustrations by Henry Fuseli and William Blake of faces and other body parts. It was successful enough to be abridged in multiple forms—“The Portable Lavater,” “Ladies’ Lavater”—and by the end of the century, Le Breton writes, “a visit to Lavater, complete with a physiognomy consultation with the master, was a must for anyone travelling to Switzerland.” The fashion spread. George Sand was a Lavater fan. So was Honoré de Balzac, who called physiognomy “prophetic.” Balzac used Lavater’s work in devising his own characters, who are practically lashed to destiny; Samuel Beckett would deride them as “clockwork cabbages.” One eighteenth-century chief justice of Naples took aside convicted prisoners who still refused to confess and personally examined their heads. If he found proof of inbuilt depravity, he would approve their execution; if he didn’t, he’d set them free.
paragraphs.zh: 现实主义回归后有一个意外后果：解剖学重新受到重视。解剖在中世纪大多处于停滞状态，但在欧洲，到了13世纪，大学课程中开始出现这门内容。文艺复兴时期，它再度繁荣，并成为艺术家学习中的常规部分，莱昂纳多的素描便是明证。与此同时，另一门同样悠久、同样痴迷人体的学问也卷土重来：相术。希腊时期以来的观点是，只要技巧足够高超，就能从一个人的外表中读出其内心的腐败倾向。他们或许能改变行为或言辞，但一副鼻子却绝不会说谎。16、17世纪，相术专著重新出现，18、19世纪则迅速流行。明星是瑞士的诗人和哲学家约翰·卡斯帕尔·拉瓦特（Johann Kaspar Lavater），他的《生理面相论片段》（Physiognomische Fragmente）是一套完整的“读面法”手册，于1775年至1778年间分四卷出版，配有亨利·芙瑟利（Henry Fuseli）和威廉·布莱克（William Blake）绘制的人脸及其他身体部位插图。它的成功以至于被改编为多种简版，如《袖珍拉瓦特》《淑女版拉瓦特》；到世纪末，勒·布里顿写道：“去一次拉瓦特，并由大师亲自进行一次相术咨询，已成为前往瑞士者必做之事。” 这种时尚便广为传播。乔治·桑（George Sand）是拉瓦特的粉丝，雨果·德·巴尔扎克（Honoré de Balzac）也是，他把相术称为“预言式的”。巴尔扎克在塑造自己的人物时使用了拉瓦特的理论，这些人物几乎都被命运捆绑；塞缪尔·贝克特（Samuel Beckett）后来讥讽他们是“发条卷心菜（clockwork cabbages）”。18世纪末，拿波里的一位首席大法官会把仍不认罪的罪犯叫到一边，亲自检查他们的头部；如果他发现有先天败坏的证据，就批准处决；如果没有，就放他们自由。

paragraphs.en: Such thinking had serious critics. If you judged someone’s soul from his appearance, Montaigne noted, it would be curtains for Socrates. The entry for “physionomie” in Diderot and d’Alembert’s Encyclopédie described it as “imaginary” and “ridiculous.” Hegel compared physiognomists to a merchant who complains that it rains whenever the fair comes to town. But the belief died hard, and photography spurred its adherents on. Once faces had been “captured,” as we still say, they could be put into giant comparative systems, proto-databases of facial types. Francis Galton, the father of eugenics, used superimposed portrait photos in his hunt for “deviant” traits that criminals supposedly shared. His friend Cesare Lombroso, an Italian criminologist, agreed, suggesting that the signs of a “born criminal” included a receding brow and a heavy jaw.
paragraphs.zh: 这种观念也有严肃的反对者。蒙田（Montaigne）指出，如果凭外表判断一个人的灵魂，那么苏格拉底就会被定为不治之症。狄德罗与达朗贝尔合编《百科全书》中的“physionomie”词条将其称为“想象的”与“荒谬的”。黑格尔把相术家比作一个总说“每当集市开到城里就会下雨”的商人。但这种信念顽固得很快不肯消失，而摄影又把它的拥趸推得更远。一旦脸孔“被捕捉”（正如我们今天仍会说的），它们就可以被放进巨大的对照体系中，形成面貌类型的早期数据库。优生学之父弗朗西斯·高尔顿（Francis Galton）便在追寻罪犯所谓“偏差”特征时，使用叠加的人像照片。其友人、意大利犯罪学家切萨雷·伦布罗索（Cesare Lombroso）也表示赞同，提出“天生罪犯”的标志包括后退的眉骨和厚重的下颌。

paragraphs.en: As genetics, psychology, and neuroscience ascended, the twentieth century sent physiognomy back into disrepute, and today, from Lavater to Lombroso, its promoters may seem a racist shade of quaint. Yet the conviction that your face will, if read skillfully, betray your hidden qualities has never gone away. The most influential facial theorist of the late twentieth century was the psychologist Paul Ekman, who argued that seven “universal emotions”—happiness, sadness, anger, surprise, disgust, fear, and contempt—recur across all cultures. Their display, he argued, can be too fleeting to control. Spot these “microexpressions” and map them, perhaps through Ekman’s proprietary Facial Action Coding System, and you’ll have a key to the human soul. Ekman’s ideas travelled unusually well: he wrote popular books, worked on the Pixar film “Inside Out” and the Fox series “Lie to Me,” and provided training to Scotland Yard, the T.S.A., the F.B.I., and the C.I.A. The appeal is plain enough. It promises a portable science of human disclosure. The trouble is that there is no context-free understanding of a social phenomenon; every use of a word like “anger” is imbricated in its past and helps shape its future. Ekman’s seven-point system, however useful as a package, leans toward rigidity, turning the face once again into a surface to be decoded.
paragraphs.zh: 随着遗传学、心理学与神经科学的崛起，20世纪把相术再次推入不名誉之地，如今从拉瓦特到伦布罗索，这些推手可能都显得古怪而带着种族色彩。然而，人们坚信只要读得好，你的脸会出卖你隐藏的品质，这一点却从未消失。20世纪末最有影响力的面部理论家是心理学家保罗·埃克曼（Paul Ekman），他认为“七种普遍情绪”——快乐、悲伤、愤怒、惊讶、厌恶、恐惧与蔑视——在所有文化中都会反复出现。他认为这些表情的外显有时过于短暂，无法自控。只要发现这些“微表情”并加以映射，比如通过埃克曼的专有“面部动作编码系统（FACS）”，就能拿到一把通向人类灵魂的钥匙。埃克曼的思想传播得异常顺畅：他写过通俗读物，参与了皮克斯电影《头脑特工队》（Inside Out）和福克斯电视剧《说谎者》（Lie to Me）的创作，还向苏格兰场警局、美国运输安全管理局（TSA）、联邦调查局（FBI）和中央情报局（CIA）提供培训。其吸引力不难理解，因为它承诺一种可携带的人类识别科学。问题在于，社会现象不存在脱离语境的理解；任何“愤怒”这样的词语都嵌入其历史之中，并共同塑造未来。尽管埃克曼的七点体系作为一个整体很有用，但它却倾向于僵化，再次把脸转化为需要被解码的表面。

paragraphs.en: The drive to define the face as a data point, something to be grasped and controlled, underpins the bureaucracy of the modern nation-state, in which faces are surveyed, categorized, and stored in digital banks. We show our faces when crossing the border, whether by holding out our passports or by staring into a digital camera. We carry papers that show our faces. Not coincidentally, plastic surgery has been advanced in part by governments: its pioneer, Sir Harold Gillies, fixed the faces of British soldiers who had been disfigured in the First World War, and today the U.S. Department of Defense funds face-transplant research in the United States.
paragraphs.zh: 将面孔定义为可被掌握和控制的数据点的这种驱动力，支撑着现代民族国家的官僚体系，在那里，面孔会被调查、分类并存入数字库。我们在过境时会出示面孔，无论是拿出护照，还是盯着数字摄像头。我们随身携带着显示面孔的证件。这并非偶然，部分原因在于政府也在推动整形手术的发展：这项技术的先驱哈罗德·吉利斯爵士（Sir Harold Gillies）曾为第一次世界大战中面容毁损的英国士兵修复面孔，而今天，美国国防部仍在美国资助面部移植研究。

paragraphs.en: Facial-recognition systems are now operative in moments big and small, whether you’re activating your Apple device or being watched as you march against the regime. Some defend the practice, and others shrug. But the biases of these systems suggest that physiognomy has taken a new, less explicit form. Police facial-recognition systems in the U.S. have had a disproportionately high error rate among Black people. Black Americans are also disproportionately likely to be subjected to such systems; they are overrepresented in the prison population, and in recorded recidivism rates. Their faces, in other words, can be drawn into a technologically enhanced ouroboros. And my own home, the U.K., always seems to follow its cousin’s lead. Between 2024 and 2025, our police forces almost doubled their use of the technology on pedestrians; on the day I write this sentence, there are two huge marches in London, one pro-Palestine and one anti-immigration, and both are being actively surveilled by facial-recognition technology. Since 2013, Britain has had an independent Biometrics Commissioner, and every holder of the post has called for greater oversight of these systems, but privacy advocates have argued in court that there are “no meaningful limits” on what the police can do.
paragraphs.zh: 面部识别系统现在已经渗透到生活中的大小场景：无论是你在激活苹果设备，还是你在反对当权者的游行中被监视。有人为这种做法辩护，也有人选择默认接受，但这些系统的偏见表明，相面术正在以一种不那么直白的新形式出现。美国的警用面部识别系统在黑人群体中的误识率明显偏高。黑人美国人也更容易成为这类系统的对象；他们在监狱人群和官方记录的再犯率中都被过度代表。换句话说，他们的面孔被卷入了一个由技术强化的“乌洛波洛斯”循环。而我自己的祖国——英国，似乎总是跟随美国这位“表亲”的步伐。

paragraphs.en: Many of the tech behemoths, too, have invested in facial recognition, and, for a while, they augmented it with emotion-detecting tools. Amazon, for instance, announced in 2019 that its system Rekognition could more accurately identify “all 7 emotions”—namely “Happy,” “Sad,” “Angry,” “Surprised,” “Disgusted,” “Calm,” and “Confused”—and had added a new one, “Fear.” This diverges a little from Ekman’s schema; perhaps the publicity chiefs found “Contemptuous” too unpleasant to sell. Rekognition has a broad spectrum of clients, from ticketing platforms to credit bureaus. It once sought to sell its services to Immigration and Customs Enforcement, though ICE plumped for a rival firm, Clearview AI, which is less interested in emotion than in flat identification. Clearview scrapes facial data from the web on a mass scale—at one point, it absorbed ten billion social-media photos—and has faced dozens of lawsuits and regulatory actions from American states and European countries. The future of emotion recognition, meanwhile, seems uncertain: Microsoft and Google both developed such tools but have since limited or ditched them. Maybe, outside the F.B.I.’s interrogation rooms, knowing how someone feels isn’t proving that valuable.
paragraphs.zh: 许多科技巨头也投入了面部识别技术，并且一度将其与情绪识别工具结合使用。例如，亚马逊在2019年宣布，其 Rekognition 系统能够更准确地识别“全部7种情绪”——即“高兴（Happy）”“悲伤（Sad）”“愤怒（Angry）”“惊讶（Surprised）”“厌恶（Disgusted）”“平静（Calm）”和“困惑（Confused）”，并新增了“恐惧（Fear）”。这与 Ekman 的情绪分类略有差异；或许公关负责人觉得“鄙视（Contemptuous）”这个词不适合拿来宣传。Rekognition 的客户范围很广，从售票平台到征信机构都有。它曾试图向移民与海关执法局（ICE）出售服务，但ICE最终选择了竞争对手 Clearview AI，后者更专注于身份识别而不是情绪识别。Clearview 会大规模抓取网络上的人脸数据——曾经一度吸收了100亿张社交媒体照片——并因此遭遇了美国多个州和欧洲国家的数十起诉讼和监管行动。与此同时，情绪识别技术的前景仍不明朗：微软和谷歌都曾开发此类工具，但后来都缩减或放弃了。也许在F.B.I.的讯问室之外，准确判断一个人的情绪并没有想象中那么有用。

paragraphs.en: The trend toward standardization and control has become coldly clear. And as with bureaucrats, so with beauticians: we can capture faces, sort them, approve them, reject them, modify them, and rejuvenate them. What you can measure you can try to perfect. The ancient approach to cosmetics seems like frippery next to the “looksmaxxers” of today, led by Clavicular, a razor-jawed American influencer whose commitment to surgery extends to smashing his own bones, and who has said that beauty isn’t “about sexual attraction, it’s about mathematics.”
paragraphs.zh: 走向标准化和控制的趋势已经异常清晰。就像官僚机构一样，美容行业也是如此：我们可以抓取面孔、进行分类、通过审核、否决、更改，甚至“年轻化”改造。凡是可被度量的，便可被尝试“完善”。相比今天由克莱维丘尔（Clavicular）这类“lookmaxxer”领军人物主导的“美学极致主义”，传统护肤化妆方法显得有些琐碎。克莱维丘尔是位颧骨棱角分明的美国网红，他对整形的投入甚至扩展到了粉碎自己的骨头，并且他说过，美容并不“关乎性吸引，而关乎数学”。

paragraphs.en: “Hamlet! I am thy father’s ghost. Avenge me!”
paragraphs.zh: “哈姆雷特！我是你父亲的幽灵。为我复仇！”

paragraphs.en: Cartoon by David Borchart
paragraphs.zh: David Borchart 绘制的漫画

paragraphs.en: Clavicular, though, represents merely the crunchy end point of a logic that has taken hold of affluent life. The same mathematical approach underpins platforms such as Instagram, a wet market for faces, on which users self-brand and sell themselves—and on which the most successful products are those which top the algorithmic charts. Hence the rise, in the twenty-tens, of the so-called Instagram face, on which so many visages slowly converged, altered by fillers and the like to become ethnically nebulous, big-lipped, and often, as videos indicated, hard to move. Last year, an ITV survey suggested that among Brits between the ages of eighteen and twenty-five—a group with near-total smartphone and social-media penetration—one in five had some form of tweakment. Le Breton, with some justification, compares the plastic surgeon to “a psychiatrist advising a paranoid patient to be more wary of his enemies.”
paragraphs.zh: 然而，Clavicular 只不过是已经深植于富裕生活方式的一种逻辑的“嘎吱作响”终点。正是这种数学逻辑支撑着 Instagram 等平台：它像一个面孔的湿市场，用户在上面进行自我包装并售卖自己——而最受欢迎的，恰恰是那些在算法排行榜上名列前茅的“商品”。因此在21世纪20年代出现了所谓的“Instagram 脸”，许多人在其中慢慢汇聚在同一种样貌之中：经过填充等处理后变得缺乏明确民族特征、嘴唇丰盈，且如视频所示，往往动作僵硬。去年，ITV 的一项调查显示，在18到25岁的英国人中——这一人群几乎人人拥有智能手机并深度使用社交媒体——每五个人就有一人做过某种“调整”。勒·布鲁顿在一定程度上有道理地将整形医生比作“建议偏执患者更加警惕敌人的精神科医生”

paragraphs.en: Social media needs the face because it burns attention for fuel, and the face is where I go when I look for you. But I can’t look to these platforms. I find it all so unsatisfying: the resculpted faces, the curated lives, the naked grabbing of my gaze. Something uncertain, something human, has been drained like blood from this world. There’s evidence that Botoxed people become worse at reading other people’s emotions, because when you engage with others you simulate their expressions in your own facial musculature. It’s as if we were commodifying the face, putting it under glass, turning it into a transactional token rather than a pliable living thing.
paragraphs.zh: 社交媒体需要“脸”，因为我的注意力在这里被点燃，而“脸”也是我寻找你的地方。但我不能去看这些平台上的内容。那样的东西让我极不满足：重塑后的面孔、经过剪辑的生活、对我目光的裸露式占有。某种不确定、某种人性的东西，像被抽走了血一样从这个世界里流失了。有证据表明，做过肉毒杆菌治疗的人在读懂他人情绪时更差，因为当你与他人互动时，你是在用自己的面部肌肉去模拟对方的表情。就好像我们正在把“脸”商品化，把它封存在玻璃柜里，把它变成一种交易代币，而不是一个柔韧的生命体。

paragraphs.en: That little half smile you see could be smug or coy, or even lady-killing; you need to ask which, and why, and in doing so you’re doing moral work, because you’re treating the smiler as an autonomous being whose form of life you need to grasp, however provisionally. But think of the faces we now trade most readily, in the billions every day: emoji, complete and prepackaged, limited in number, coined and issued by a consortium of leading tech companies. I can never shake the sense that they’re fatally cheap. If I saw a friend post about this piece, or send me a message about it, and use an emoji in doing so, I might first envision the real face of that friend, or try to, but then I would feel unmoored. No true face is as standardizable as the image the person has sent in its stead, and another friend, my partner, even my boss might have sent me the exact same little face earlier. It can’t represent them all. It’s like canned laughter, like a cliché. Yes, we know that emoji are useful lies, saving us time and dodging the difficulty of putting emotions into words; still, in so dodging, we falsify something essential about what our real faces can do. The emoji could be the culmination of everything we’ve done to the face over the centuries, all the multiplication and commodification and repacking. We’ve made it trivial.
paragraphs.zh: 你看到的那点半笑容，可能是自鸣得意，也可能是媚态百出，甚至是致命的“迷人”，你需要判断它是什么、为什么是那样，而在判断的过程中，你在完成一种道德劳动：你把那个笑的人当成一个有自主性的存在，把他/她的生存方式——哪怕只是暂时地——理解出来。但想一想我们如今每天最容易交换的那些“脸”：表情符号，完整且预包装，数量有限，由一群头部科技公司联合“铸造”并发行。我始终无法摆脱它们注定廉价的感觉。如果有朋友在这里发了这篇文章，或发消息告诉我这篇文章，并且用表情符号来表达，我可能先去想象那位朋友真实的脸，或者试图这样做，但很快会感到失去了依托。没有任何真实的脸能像对方发来的这张小图那样被标准化，而且可能另一位朋友、我的伴侣，甚至我的老板早已给我发过完全一样的小脸。它不可能代表他们每一个人。这就像罐头笑声，像一个陈词滥调。是的，我们知道表情符号是有用的“谎言”，它节省了时间，绕过了把情绪翻译成语言的困难；但在这么做的同时，我们也扭曲了真实面孔所能承载的某种核心能力。表情符号可能是我们几个世纪以来对面孔所做一切加工的顶点：不断扩张、商品化，再次打包。我们已经把它变得过于简单了。

paragraphs.en: This isn’t what I want for faces. I want them to shift, to resist, to allure. I find myself thinking of a photograph taken by Peter Hujar in 1975: a black-and-white portrait of the poet and dance critic Edwin Denby, at the age of seventy-two. His eyes are shut, his mouth neither smiling nor frowning.
paragraphs.zh: 这不是我想要的面孔。我希望它们有变化、能抗拒、能吸引。我常想到1975年彼得·休贾尔拍摄的一张照片：一张黑白人像，拍的是诗人兼舞评家埃德温·登比，他当时七十二岁。双眼紧闭，嘴巴既不在微笑，也不在皱眉。

paragraphs.en: The picture tempts you to suppose that you can read Denby’s face, that he’s experiencing fatigue, or stress, or agitation. Or maybe he’s just world-weary. But if you learn that Denby suffered, in his later years, from depression, and would kill himself some years later, you might see something like pain. Then you might learn a little about Hujar, too, and about the way he liked to concentrate on the emotions of his subjects. You might be less inclined to assume that a feeling has been casually captured, as it might be in a family snap. By this point, the question the image first posed, “What is this man feeling?,” seems as urgent as ever, perhaps even more charged. Yet every attempt to answer it has led nowhere. Denby sits there forever; his troubles are radiant.
paragraphs.zh: 这张照片似乎在诱使你以为自己可以读懂登比的脸——他似乎在疲惫，或者紧张、烦躁。或者他只是厌世了。但如果你得知登比晚年曾罹患抑郁，几年后也选择了自杀，你也许会看到某种痛苦。随后你又可能进一步了解休贾尔，以及他如何喜欢专注捕捉被摄者情绪的方式。你可能就不再轻易认为这是一种“像家庭合影那样偶然”捕捉到的情感。到这时，图片最初提出的问题“这个人此刻在感受什么？”仍然迫切得甚至更具张力。然而每一次试图回答都无疾而终。登比就那样静静坐着；他的困扰却在散发着光芒。

paragraphs.en: Here’s another way of approaching that image. Denby, as a critic, was fascinated by the great dancer Vaslav Nijinsky, as much for his expressions as for his torso, arms, or legs. “The manner in which Nijinsky’s face changes from role to role is immediately striking,” Denby wrote, in 1943. “It is enhanced by makeup, but not created by it. In fact, a friend pointed out that the only role in which one recognises Nijinsky’s civilian face is that of Petrouchka, where he is most heavily made up.” It was as if the images held several traits in suspense. Many people who met Nijinsky were enthralled by that curious face. Stravinsky recalled that it “could become the most powerful actor’s mask I have ever seen.” It was part of Nijinsky’s weird magic, whether he was embodying a sex-hungry faun or a wooden puppet come to life: he drew other personae out of himself.
paragraphs.zh: 还有一种看待那张照片的方法。作为评论家，登比对伟大的舞者瓦斯拉夫·尼金斯基迷恋不已，不仅因为他的躯干、手臂和腿，更因为他的表情。1943年，登比写道：“尼金斯基的面孔从一个角色转向另一个角色时变化之明显，真是令人震撼。”他写道：“这些变化虽因妆容而更突出，却并非由妆容创造。实际上，有一位朋友指出，唯一能认出尼金斯基平日容貌的是《彼得鲁奇卡》中的角色，因为他在此时妆容最重。”就像这些影像中悬置着多个特征。许多见过尼金斯基的人都被那张奇异的脸深深吸引。斯特拉文斯基回忆道，它“可能是我见过最有力量的演员面具”。无论他是在演绎渴望性爱的野人，还是活过来的木偶——那都是尼金斯基怪异的魅力之一：他能从自己身上抽取出其他人格。

paragraphs.en: After Nijinsky began to show signs of schizophrenia, this mask, or persona, grew disturbed. He seemed to recede into his eyes. His diary, written during two feverish months in 1919, teems with scribbled drawings of eyes, giant and black. He believed that he was being watched, which for most of his life had been true. One winter night, in the late nineteen-twenties, he was taken out of an asylum in Paris by his former boss, the Ballets Russes’ Serge Diaghilev, to attend a production of “Petrouchka,” in which the title role was now performed by a younger man, Serge Lifar. Throughout the evening, Nijinsky barely spoke. He just stared. The diplomat Harry Kessler, who was there, said that his “big eyes” were like those of “a sick animal”; the dancer Tamara Karsavina caught him straining to look at her face, then, when she met his gaze, he “turned his head like a child that wants to hide tears.”
paragraphs.zh: 在尼金斯基开始出现精神分裂迹象后，这副面具，或者说人格，变得紊乱起来。他似乎退缩进了自己的眼睛。1919年他在两个月发热般的疯狂时期所写的日记中，充满了巨大的黑色眼睛涂鸦。那时他确信自己正在被注视，这在他一生的大多数时候都是真的。有一个冬夜，在1920年代末，他被巴黎的前上司、俄国芭蕾舞团经理谢尔盖·迪亚基列夫从精神病院接出来，去观看一场《彼得鲁奇卡》演出，而主角如今由一位更年轻的舞者谢拉夫·利法尔出演。整场演出里，尼金斯基几乎没说话，只是凝视着。外交官哈里·凯斯勒当时在场，说他的“巨眼”像“病态动物的眼睛”；舞者塔玛拉·卡萨维娜看到他正使劲看向她的脸，随后当他与她对上眼神时，他“转过头去，像一个想把泪水藏起来的孩子”。

paragraphs.en: A photograph of the group survives from that night. Although Lifar was the star of the show, all the faces are turned to Nijinsky, whose gaze is directed offstage. His eyes and smile are too wide, and his right hand grasps the air. This face conveys a need, but a need that eluded everyone; it eludes me now, as I look at the picture, though I know it’s there. And, looking again at Hujar’s portrait of Denby, I see something similar. Another man, engulfed in feelings, shows his face to the world. His eyes are closed; his expression seems charged. I wish I knew how he felt. It seems important that I do. All I know is that I cannot look away. ♦
paragraphs.zh: 那晚的这张团体照片得以留下。尽管利法尔是当晚的主角，但所有人的视线都集中到尼金斯基身上，他的目光投向了台下。他的眼睛和笑容都显得过于张扬，右手则在空中抓握。这样的表情传达出一种需求，但这种需求当时让每个人都抓不住；而如今当我再次凝视这张照片时，它仍然让我无法读懂，尽管我知道那种感觉确实存在。再看一次胡贾尔绘制的丹比肖像时，我也看到了类似的东西。另一个男人被情绪淹没，把自己的脸交给世界看。他闭上了眼，表情似乎充满了力量。我希望自己知道他当时的感受。我知道这一点很重要。到最后，我只知道自己无法移开目光。♦
